senses.definition: Rules or limits that control what people can do or what is allowed.
examples: There are strict restrictions on smoking inside the building.
Travel restrictions were introduced during the health crisis.
The new law places restrictions on the use of personal data.

senses.definition: Limiting what people can do or what is possible, often more than is wanted.
examples: Some students find the school rules too restrictive.
The company has a very restrictive dress code.
The new policy is highly restrictive for small businesses.

senses.definition: Describing a grammar structure that limits or defines the meaning of the word it follows.
examples: In this sentence, the phrase after the noun is restrictive.
A restrictive clause gives essential information about the noun.
You do not use commas around a restrictive relative clause.

senses.definition: In a way that limits what is allowed or possible.
examples: The rules are interpreted very restrictively by the authorities.
The contract was written rather restrictively, leaving little room for change.
Some critics say the law is being applied too restrictively.

senses.definition: In grammar, used to show that a word or phrase limits the meaning of another word.
examples: Here the relative clause is used restrictively to identify which students you mean.
The adjective is used restrictively to narrow the class of objects described.

senses.definition: The quality of limiting people or things too much.
examples: Many workers complained about the restrictiveness of the new schedule.
We need to reduce the restrictiveness of these regulations.
The restrictiveness of the rules discouraged innovation.

senses.definition: Plural of restrictiveness; several different kinds or degrees of being restrictive.
examples: The study compared the restrictivenesses of various immigration systems.
Different policy areas show different restrictivenesses over time.

senses.definition: Grammar structures, especially clauses or phrases, that limit the meaning of the nouns they describe.
examples: Writers must learn to distinguish restrictives from non-restrictives.
In this paragraph, most of the relative clauses are restrictives.

senses.definition: Limits what someone can do or what is possible.
examples: The school restricts the use of mobile phones in class.
The new rule restricts parking to residents only.
Bad weather often restricts outdoor activities.

senses.definition: To strike something again, especially to make a new coin or medal from an old die.
examples: The mint decided to restrike the popular gold coin for collectors.
Old medals were restruck using the original tools.
They plan to restrike the coin series next year.

senses.definition: A coin, medal, or similar object that is made later from an original die, not at the time of the first issue.
examples: This piece is a modern restrike of an older silver dollar.
Restrikes are usually less valuable than original issues.
The museum displayed both the original coin and the restrike.

senses.definition: Third person singular form of restrike: strikes again, especially to make a new coin or medal from an old die.
examples: The mint occasionally restrikes this design for investors.
The company restrikes popular medals when demand rises.

senses.definition: Plural of restrike: coins or medals made later from original dies.
examples: The collection includes several nineteenth-century restrikes.
Many restrikes are sold specifically to collectors.

senses.definition: Present participle of restrike: striking again, especially making coins or medals again with the same design.
examples: The mint is restriking the famous coin series this year.
By restriking old designs, they make them available to new collectors.

senses.definition: The act or process of striking coins or medals again from existing designs.
examples: The restriking of classic coins increased their popularity.
Restriking allows mints to meet collector demand without changing the design.

senses.definition: To put new strings on something like a musical instrument, a racket, or beads.
examples: I need to restring my guitar before the concert.
She took her tennis racket to the shop to be restrung.
The jeweller offered to restring the broken necklace.

senses.definition: To limit or confine something; an old or rare form related to restrict.
examples: The ancient law sought to restringe the power of local rulers.
Poets sometimes used archaic verbs like "restringe" to give a classical tone.

senses.definition: Simple past form of restring, especially in non-standard or older use: put new strings on something.
examples: He restringed his guitar the night before the show.
She carefully restringed the old violin for her grandson.

senses.definition: A non-standard spelling of restringing: putting new strings on something.
examples: While restringeing the guitar, he broke another string.
She spent the afternoon restringeing several tennis rackets.

senses.definition: The quality of being strict or tight; an extremely rare or technical word.
examples: Some critics argued about the restringency of the new safety standards.
The restringency of the rules left little room for interpretation.

senses.definition: Very strict or tight in effect; a very rare or technical word.
examples: The committee adopted restringent measures to control spending.
Such restringent rules can discourage new applicants.

senses.definition: Plural of restringent; strict rules or measures, in very rare use.
examples: Various fiscal restringents were introduced during the crisis.
These legal restringents shaped corporate behaviour for decades.

senses.definition: A person or tool that puts new strings on musical instruments, rackets, or similar items.
examples: He works as a professional guitar restringer.
The tennis club hired a full-time restringer for members' rackets.

senses.definition: Third person singular form of restringe; limits or confines something, in archaic use.
examples: The strict code restringes their personal freedom.
His duty restringes his actions more than he wishes.

senses.definition: Present participle of restring: putting new strings on something.
examples: He is restringing his guitar in the next room.
Restringing a racket regularly can improve your game.

senses.definition: The act of putting new strings on an instrument, racket, or similar object.
examples: Guitar restringing is recommended every few months.
The shop offers same-day restringing for tennis players.

senses.definition: Puts new strings on something, usually as a regular activity.
examples: He restrings his guitar every few weeks.
The shop restrings hundreds of rackets each season.

senses.definition: To put new stripes or markings on something, especially a road or a vehicle.
examples: The city plans to restrip the main roads this summer.
They had to restrip the parking lot after repaving.

senses.definition: To try hard again; to make a new effort after failing before.
examples: After several setbacks, she chose to restrive rather than give up.
We must restrive for peace despite past disappointments.

senses.definition: Past participle of restrive; having tried again, in archaic or literary style.
examples: Long had they restriven to change their fate.
Having often restriven, he finally achieved his goal.

senses.definition: Third person singular form of restrive: tries hard again.
examples: Each time he fails, he restrives with fresh energy.
The community restrives for justice year after year.

senses.definition: Present participle of restrive: trying hard again.
examples: Restriving after failure can lead to unexpected success.
She kept restriving despite many difficulties.

senses.definition: To strike again, for example in printing, painting, or minting.
examples: The printer had to restroke the type to make a clearer impression.
The artist decided to restroke parts of the painting.

senses.definition: A room with a toilet and a sink in a public place, such as a restaurant, shop, or airport.
examples: Excuse me, where is the restroom?
He went to the restroom to wash his hands.
The restrooms in this mall are very clean.

senses.definition: Plural of restroom: several rooms with toilets and sinks in a public place.
examples: The restrooms are located at the end of the hall.
This stadium has many restrooms for visitors.

senses.definition: Old or rare past form of restrive or restrive: tried hard again.
examples: Though he failed, he restrove to finish the task.
They restrove against the storm until they reached safety.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of restrike: struck again, especially to make coins or medals again.
examples: The mint restruck the coin using the original dies.
Several famous medals were restruck in the twentieth century.

senses.definition: To change the way something is organized, especially a company, system, or process, to make it work better.
examples: The company plans to restructure its sales department.
The government is restructuring the tax system.
We had to restructure the project after the budget was cut.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of restructure: changed the organization of something.
examples: They restructured the company to cut costs.
The debt was restructured over a longer period.

senses.definition: Changed in form or organization, especially to work better.
examples: The restructured team is now more efficient.
A restructured loan can help struggling homeowners.

senses.definition: Third person singular form of restructure: changes the organization of something.
examples: The company regularly restructures its operations.
The bank restructures debt for customers who are in trouble.

senses.definition: Present participle of restructure: changing the way something is organized.
examples: They are restructuring the company after the merger.
We are restructuring our courses to better meet students' needs.

senses.definition: The process of changing the organization of a company, system, or plan.
examples: The restructuring of the company led to many job losses.
Debt restructuring can help a country avoid default.
The school is undergoing a major restructuring.

senses.definition: Plural of restructuring: several changes in the organization of companies, systems, or plans.
examples: The last decade saw many corporate restructurings.
Debt restructurings are common during financial crises.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of restring: put new strings on something.
examples: He had his guitar restrung before the performance.
I restrung my tennis racket last week.

senses.definition: Stops working or moving in order to relax, sleep, or recover strength.
examples: She usually rests for an hour after lunch.
The doctor says he rests too little on weekends.

senses.definition: All the remaining people or things that have not been mentioned.
examples: You can have these books, and I will take the rests.
She finished half the work today and will do the rests tomorrow.

senses.definition: Studied something again, often more carefully.
examples: She restudied her notes before the final exam.
The scientists restudied the data and found a mistake.

senses.definition: Third person singular form of restudy: studies something again.
examples: He often restudies difficult chapters before tests.
The team restudies its strategy after every match.

senses.definition: Plural of restudy: pieces of research or study done again.
examples: Several restudies confirmed the original results.
The article discusses restudies of classic experiments.

senses.definition: To study something again, often to understand it better or correct mistakes.
examples: I need to restudy this chapter before tomorrow's quiz.
They decided to restudy the case after new evidence appeared.
You should restudy your grammar notes regularly.

senses.definition: The act of studying something again, often as part of research.
examples: A restudy of the problem produced different results.
The book is a restudy of traditional theories.

senses.definition: Present participle of restudy: studying again.
examples: She is restudying the textbook for the exam.
Restudying difficult topics can improve your grades.

senses.definition: To put new filling inside something soft, such as a toy, cushion, or seat.
examples: She decided to restuff the old sofa cushions.
The teddy bear was torn, so they restuffed it.
You can restuff your pillow to make it firmer.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of restuff: put new filling inside something soft.
examples: We restuffed the armchairs instead of buying new ones.
The toy was carefully restuffed and stitched up.

senses.definition: Present participle of restuff: putting new filling inside something soft.
examples: She is restuffing the sofa cushions in the living room.
Restuffing old furniture can be cheaper than replacing it.

senses.definition: The act of putting new filling inside cushions, furniture, or toys.
examples: The service includes cleaning and restuffing of pillows.
Furniture restuffing can extend the life of a sofa.

senses.definition: Puts new filling inside something soft, usually as a repeated activity.
examples: The company restuffs old furniture for customers.
He restuffs his favourite pillow every year.

senses.definition: In cricket, to put the stumps back in place after they have been knocked over.
examples: The umpire stopped the game to restump the wicket.
They had to restump the stumps before play could continue.

senses.definition: To replace or repair the supporting stumps under a house or building.
examples: We need to restump the old house to make it safe.
The building was restumped to fix structural problems.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of restump: had the stumps replaced in cricket or the supports renewed under a building.
examples: The umpire restumped the wicket after the bails were knocked off.
They restumped the house last year to prevent further damage.

senses.definition: Present participle of restump: replacing stumps in cricket or renewing the supports under a building.
examples: They are restumping the house to meet safety standards.
Restumping the wicket took only a few seconds.

senses.definition: The process of replacing or repairing the supports under a building.
examples: House restumping can be expensive but is often necessary.
They specialise in restumping and raising old homes.

senses.definition: Third person singular form of restump: replaces cricket stumps or building supports.
examples: The groundsman quickly restumps the wicket between overs.
The company restumps several houses each month.
